The Solar Neighborhood¶

The Solar Neighborhood can be roughly defined as all of the objects within 10 pc of the Sun.

  • Objects within 10 pc of the Sun will have parallax values greater than 100 mas.

Create and run a Gaia query to find a high quality complete sample of all of the objects Gaia surveyed within the Solar Neighborhood.

  • Your sample should only have high quality data (parallax SNR > 15)
  • All of your objects should have bp-rp colors and radial velocity measurements.

You will want the columns: source_id, bp_rp, parallax, parallax_over_error, pmra, pmdec, radial_velocity

Don't print out the entire object table!

In [4]:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.image as mpimg

import numpy as np
import astropy.units as u

from astroquery.simbad import Simbad

from astropy.table import QTable
from astroquery.gaia import Gaia

Warning: the passwords of all user accounts have been inactivated.              Please visit https://cas.cosmos.esa.int/cas/passwd to set a new one. Workaround solutions for the Gaia Archive issues following the infrastructure upgrade: https://www.cosmos.esa.int/web/gaia/news#WorkaroundArchive
In [5]:
my_query = f"""
SELECT TOP 500
source_id, bp_rp, parallax, parallax_over_error, pmra, pmdec, radial_velocity
FROM gaiadr3.gaia_source_lite
WHERE parallax > 100
AND bp_rp IS NOT NULL
AND radial_velocity IS NOT NULL
ORDER BY parallax DESC
"""
In [6]:
my_job_query = Gaia.launch_job_async(my_query)

INFO: Query finished. [astroquery.utils.tap.core]
In [7]:
print(my_job_query)

<Table length=216>
        name         dtype    unit                              description                            
------------------- ------- -------- ------------------------------------------------------------------
          source_id   int64          Unique source identifier (unique within a particular Data Release)
              bp_rp float32      mag                                                     BP - RP colour
           parallax float64      mas                                                           Parallax
parallax_over_error float32                                      Parallax divided by its standard error
               pmra float64 mas / yr                         Proper motion in right ascension direction
              pmdec float64 mas / yr                             Proper motion in declination direction
    radial_velocity float32   km / s                                                    Radial velocity

Phase: COMPLETED
Owner: None

In [8]:
my_table = my_job_query.get_results()
my_table[0:5]

Make a plot of pmra vs pmdec for the objects in the Solar Neighborhood¶

  • Adjust your values for the X and Y limits so the center of the plot is (0,0)
  • Make the points easy to see
  • Label the axes
In [9]:
np.max(my_table['pmdec']), np.min(my_table['pmdec'])
Out[9]:
(np.float64(10362.394206546573), np.float64(-5817.8001940492695))
In [10]:
np.max(my_table['pmra']), np.min(my_table['pmra'])
Out[10]:
(np.float64(6765.995136250774), np.float64(-4406.469178827325))
In [11]:
fig, ax = plt.subplots(
    figsize = (8, 8), 
    constrained_layout = True
)

ax.set_ylim(-11000,11000)
ax.set_xlim(-7500,7500)

ax.tick_params(axis='both', which='major', labelsize = 18)

###

ax.set_xlabel("PM Ra",
              fontfamily = 'serif',
              fontsize = 25) 

ax.set_ylabel("PM Dec",
              fontfamily = 'serif',
              fontsize = 25)


ax.vlines(0, -11000, 11000,
          color = 'Orchid',
          linewidth = 3,
          linestyle = '--')

ax.hlines(0, -11000, 11000,
          color = 'Orchid',
          linewidth = 3,
          linestyle = '--')

ax.plot(my_table['pmra'], my_table['pmdec'],
        color = "DarkOrchid",
        marker = "*",
        linestyle = "None",
        markersize = 5
       );

Make a histogram of the values of the radial velocity.¶

  • Use bin sizes of 10 km/s
  • Label the axes
In [12]:
np.max(my_table['radial_velocity']), np.min(my_table['radial_velocity'])
Out[12]:
(np.float32(245.05313), np.float32(-414.01544))
In [13]:
my_bins = np.arange(-420,250,10)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(
    figsize = (10, 5), 
    constrained_layout = True
)

ax.set_xlabel("Radial Velocity (km/s)",
              fontfamily = 'serif',
              fontsize = 15)

ax.set_ylabel("Number of Stars",
              fontfamily = 'serif',
              fontsize = 15)

ax.hist(my_table['radial_velocity'],
        bins = my_bins,
        histtype = 'stepfilled',
        facecolor = 'MediumOrchid');

Calculate the total space velocity for the Solar Neighborhood objects¶

In [14]:
my_table['Total_PM'] = np.sqrt(my_table['pmra'] ** 2 + my_table['pmdec'] ** 2)
In [15]:
my_table['Tan_Velocity'] = 4.74047 * ( my_table['Total_PM'] / my_table['parallax'] )
In [16]:
my_table['Space_Velocity'] = np.sqrt(my_table['Tan_Velocity'] ** 2 + my_table['radial_velocity'] ** 2)

Make a histogram of the values of the total space velocity.¶

  • Use bin sizes of 10 km/s
  • Label the axes
In [17]:
np.max(my_table['Space_Velocity']), np.min(my_table['Space_Velocity'])
Out[17]:
(np.float64(415.8951282492758), np.float64(4.569050703620665))
In [18]:
my_bins2 = np.arange(0,420,10)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(
    figsize = (10, 5), 
    constrained_layout = True
)

my_bins=10

ax.set_xlabel("Total Space Velocity (km/s)",
              fontfamily = 'serif',
              fontsize = 15)

ax.set_ylabel("Number of Stars",
              fontfamily = 'serif',
              fontsize = 15)

ax.hist(my_table['Space_Velocity'],
        bins = my_bins2,
        histtype = 'stepfilled',
        facecolor = 'MediumOrchid');

Speedy Object¶

You should notice that one of the objects has a very high total space velocity.

Print out the following sentence for the high velocity object.

The object with the highest space velocity in the solar neighborhood is OBJECT_ID with a velocity of VELOCITY km/s and a parallax of PARALLAX mas. The color of the object is BP_RP which is within BP_RP - SOLAR_COLOR mags of the Sun's color.

  • The Sun has a bp_rp value of 0.82
  • Use f-string formatting to make your output look nice.
  • All floats should be printed to one decimal place.
  • Your output should NOT be all be all on one line that needs to be scrolled through.
  • Replace the CAP values with data. DO NOT HARDCODE!! - use variables.
In [19]:
idx = np.argmax(my_table['Space_Velocity'])

high_v_obj = my_table[idx]
In [24]:
my_string=f"""The object with the highest space velocity in the solar neighborhood is {high_v_obj['source_id']:.1f}
with a velocity of {high_v_obj['Space_Velocity']:.1f} km/s and a parallax of {high_v_obj['parallax']:.1f} mas.
The color of the object is {high_v_obj['bp_rp']:.1f} which is within {(high_v_obj['bp_rp'] - 0.82):.1f} mags of the Sun's color."""

print(my_string)

The object with the highest space velocity in the solar neighborhood is 3320184202856435712.0
with a velocity of 415.9 km/s and a parallax of 123.2 mas.
The color of the object is 0.8 which is within 0.0 mags of the Sun's color.

Pretty Pictures - Pick an Object¶

Pick an interesting-to-you (non-Solar System) object.¶

  • Here is a good place for ideas¶

  • The Arp catalog is a particularly good list for the more decerning tastes.¶

  • Don't pick a field with just one bright star in it (i.e Sirius).¶

The STScI Digitized Sky Survey¶

  • Download a GIF images of your object
  • Adjust the width and height so that you can see the entire object with a little context
  • Pick the Survey that looks best to you
  • Upload the GIF of your object to this directory
  • Plot up the image
In [25]:
my_field_image = mpimg.imread("./NGC2685.gif")
In [26]:
fig, ax = plt.subplots(
    figsize = (15, 15), 
    constrained_layout = True
)

ax.imshow(my_field_image, cmap = plt.cm.gray);
